results.possibleIdentity: Murray Zangen was a research engineer and computer scientist active in New York City during the late 1960s and 1970s. He was employed by the Riverside Research Institute (RRI), a non-profit scientific research organization established in 1967 as a spin-off from Columbia University's Electronics Research Laboratories. At RRI, Zangen specialized in technical research and engineering, contributing to the institute's work in fields such as computer programming and data analysis. He is notably recognized for his collaborations with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a service organization that matched artists with technical experts. Zangen appeared in E.A.T. records as a technical resource and research engineer available for artist collaborations. Professionally, he co-authored several scientific papers with researchers from the Albert Einstein College of Medicine (AECOM) and Montefiore Hospital, including 'Analysis of a Mnemonic Device' (1970) and 'CLUSTER: A program for identifying recurrent clusters' (1976), the latter of which described a computer program designed to analyze memory and data patterns.
